philosophical-yap-sessions.title: The Pinocchio Paradox
philosophical-yap-sessions.subtitle: In which me and my friend Marco try to discuss whether Pinocchio is telling the truth... over 1L of sake.
philosophical-yap-sessions.excerpt:
The paradox goes as follows—Pinocchio proclaims: "My nose grows now."

Let us assume two scenarios: one where Pinocchio is telling the truth, and one where he is lying.

In the scenario where Pinocchio is telling the truth, his nose should grow. Yet, dear reader, we know that Pinocchio's nose only grows...
philosophical-yap-sessions.body:
The paradox goes as follows—Pinocchio proclaims: "My nose grows now."

Let us assume two scenarios: one where Pinocchio is telling the truth, and one where he is lying.

In the scenario where Pinocchio is telling the truth, his nose should grow. Yet, dear reader, we know that Pinocchio's nose only grows when he is lying. So he must have been telling a lie when he said the statement, "My nose grows now." But if he was telling a lie, then his statement should be false; his nose should not grow now. This seems to rule out the possibility that Pinocchio was telling the truth.

Yet imagine the scenario where Pinocchio is lying. He proclaims: "My nose grows now." Since he is lying, his nose should grow. Yet, if his nose grows, and he claims that his nose will grow, then he is by definition telling the truth. How, then, could he possibly be lying?

Over a liter of sake, me and my friend Marco came up with two solution to this problem: we must pay attention to Pinocchio's internal state, and we must consider time as a factor. Let me explain.

Here is the first elegant solution to the Pinocchio paradox. In the situation where Pinocchio proclaims the actuality that his nose grows (i.e. wherein he proclaims that his nose will grow and it does indeed grow), it is possible that Pinocchio simply does not *believe* his nose will grow, yet says that it will grow. In this case, he has lied even though his statement is in fact correct, and so it is consistent with the fact that his nose has grown. Similar to how I might say, "It will rain tomorrow," despite my not believing it will rain. As it turns out, it *does* rain tomorrow, contrary to my initial belief. Thus, I have still lied (i.e. my internal belief regarding the statement was false), yet my statement was in fact true.

Here is another elegant solution: Pinocchio understands that his nose might grow, but *not right this very moment*. In this case, on a strict interpretation of the phrase, "My nose grows now," he is in fact lying. Once Pinocchio tells the lie that his nose will grow immediately, his nose subsequently grows, because his statement was in fact false.

What are your thoughts on me and Marco's solutions to this problem? I'd love to hear your feedback at jakes-blog.selection732@silomails.com.

book-and-media-reviews.title: Shawshank Redemption
book-and-media-reviews.subtitle: Hope and Rebellion
book-and-media-reviews.excerpt: I recently re-watched Shawshank Redemption, and am reminded once again why this is, in my opinion, one of the best films ever made. While there are many aspects of this film that make it great—such as the acting, character development, and the honest examination of prison life—today I want to focus on what is arguably the most significant aspect of the film—the theme of hope; specifically, hope for something *better*, hope for the emancipation of the spirit, despite the overwhelming evidence against the contrary...
book-and-media-reviews.body:
I recently re-watched Shawshank Redemption, and am reminded once again why this is, in my opinion, one of the best films ever made. While there are many aspects of this film that make it great—such as the acting, character development, and the honest examination of prison life—today I want to focus on what is arguably the most significant aspect of the film—the theme of hope; specifically, hope for something *better*, hope for the emancipation of the spirit, despite the overwhelming evidence against the contrary. But first, let us go over a plot outline for the film.

(Warning: spoilers abound!)

The film centers around Andy Dufresne, a man imprisoned for murdering his wife and her lover, and Elis "Red" Redding, another man convicted of murder. While life on the inside is initially extremely tough for Andy, he eventually curries favor with the guards by providing them with financial advice. Eventually, through his persistence and refusal to submit himself to the mundanity of prison life, he helps to build a library for the inmates for their enjoyment. Eventually, he even manages to help many others get their high school equivalencies, preparing them for stepping away from a life of crime once they're released from prison.

There is, however, one major catch to Andy's predicament—Andy is in fact innocent of the crime he was accused of committing, as revealed by one of his fellow inmates who heard a confession by the actual murderer. Andy delivers this news to the prison warden, who shuts him down and kills the fellow inmate who could have provided the evidence for overturning Andy's conviction.

Under these circumstances, most anyone—myself included—might have given up. Indeed, Andy later asks a fellow inmate for a length of rope, and it is heavily implied that he will use this to hang himself. But Andy being Andy, he has other plans, and those plans involve a prison escape. In a wonderful scene, in which Andy crawls through sewage to reach freedom, he exposes the corruption going on at Shawshank Prison by mailing revealing documents to the press, and Warden Norton kills himself. Red, inspired by Andy's philosophy of hope, gets his parole approved and reunites with Andy off the coast of Mexico.

Alright, now that that's out of the way, it's time to talk about my favorite subject—philosophy. Andy and Red are essentially on two ends of what I will call the Spectrum of Hope. First and foremost, it's useful to define what is meant by the term "hope". In the traditional sense, "hope" might be defined as the positive expectation for something, be it a prospective new partner, a pay raise, or simply the desire to find an object we were looking for. But the idea of hope in the film is not so simple. Because for Andy, "hope" does not simply mean the hope of one day getting out of prison, but is more so an orientation towards life. One particular scene in the movie comes to mind, when talking about this, which is the famous opera scene.

Upon receiving donations from his locality for a list of donations for his library, Andy rebels against the prison establishment by playing an opera song over the PA system. (It truly is a wonderful scene, and I encourage you to watch it here: https://youtu.be/Bjqmg_7J53s). Being the first time the inmates had heard any music in years, the whole prison stops to admire the music. After spending two weeks in solitary confinement, Andy returns to tell his fellow inmates that it was the easiest time in solitary he had ever done, because the music inspired him in a particular way. When pressed by Red about what precise feeling he is referring to, Andy replies it was hope that kept him going. Surely, Andy does not mean that he hopes he will be let out of solitary sooner than expected, or that the experience will not be as bad as he imagined it would be. Thus, taken in this context, hope is not merely the expectation for something good. What, then, does Andy mean?

I think Andy's use of the word "hope" can be taken to mean something like this: it is the refusal to let your circumstances define you. It is the blanket rejection of objectivity, the denial that you are what society condemns you to be.

Indeed, in many ways, Andy defies expectations for what might be expected of him in his situation. To have both the courage and persistence to build a chess set from polishing rocks, let alone building an entire library for the inmates—this takes a level of fortitude that I'm not sure I have have. But it is a character strength, a virtue, that all of us can aspire to—to never give up, to *deny causality itself*. It is something of which I have much to learn from Andy Dufresne.

What are your thoughts on my analysis of hope in Shawshank Redemption? Feel free to let me know your thoughts at jakes-blog.selection732@silomails.com.

kyle-the-dog.title: I smell something nice over there...
kyle-the-dog.subtitle: In which Kyle tries to discern what smells so god awfully nice.
kyle-the-dog.excerpt:
"Something smells nice over there...

Could it be? No, impossible! A human ordered a blue-rare steak!"
kyle-the-dog.body:
"Something smells nice over there...

Could it be? No, impossible! A human ordered a blue-rare steak!"

kyle-the-dog.title: Being A Dog Is Difficult
kyle-the-dog.subtitle: In which Kyle discusses the various struggles he faces as the world's cutest canine.
kyle-the-dog.excerpt: "What's so hard about being a dog? To be so cute that humans want to pick you up all the time. It gets tiring, man! How would you like it if a giant picked you up and didn't let you go *twenty times a day*?!"
kyle-the-dog.body: "What's so hard about being a dog? To be so cute that humans want to pick you up all the time. It gets tiring, man! How would you like it if a giant picked you up and didn't let you go *twenty times a day*?!"

kyle-the-dog.title: Kyle The Dog Greets You!
kyle-the-dog.subtitle: In which Kyle says, "Hello!"
kyle-the-dog.excerpt:
"Oh hey there, friend! Welcome to my page.

In here, you'll find me talking about eggs, sweet potatoes, and long walks near the harbour front.

We'll also talk about the meaning of unconditional love, like how I unconditionally love the smell of cooked crabs...
kyle-the-dog.body:
"Oh hey there, friend! Welcome to my page.

In here, you'll find me talking about eggs, sweet potatoes, and long walks near the harbour front.

We'll also talk about the meaning of unconditional love, like how I unconditionally love the smell of cooked crabs.

Anyway, hope you enjoy my rants! See you back here soon!

-Kyle"

reflections-from-a-mountaintop.title: Hiking as Escape from Society
reflections-from-a-mountaintop.subtitle: In which I discuss the value of hiking as an escape from the pressures of modern life.
reflections-from-a-mountaintop.excerpt: Hiking will always occupy a special place in my heart. And I don't count hikes on flat terrain—those are for weaklings. Sure, one might plausibly call hiking on a mountain walking with extra steps (literally), but I would argue there is something special and distinct about hiking that sets it apart from a walk in the park. That being said, let's explore what makes hiking so different from any other type of exercise one could do...
reflections-from-a-mountaintop.body:
Hiking will always occupy a special place in my heart. And I don't count hikes on flat terrain—those are for weaklings. Sure, one might plausibly call hiking on a mountain walking with extra steps (literally), but I would argue there is something special and distinct about hiking that sets it apart from a walk in the park. That being said, let's explore what makes hiking so different from any other type of exercise one could do.

### Hiking as Purgatory between Civilization and the Wild
I like to think of hiking as existing somewhere between civilization and pure wilderness. To hike is to place yourself between mountains, to walk a barely-paved path to get to a destination through a deliberately inefficient means. (Incidentally, this is roughly the definition that Bernard Suits gives for the idea of a "game". More on that in another blog post.) For example, to get from Aberdeen to Wan Chai in Hong Kong Island, there are many routes that are not only faster, but more efficient (in terms of distance, energy expenditure, etc.). Not only that, some hikes are in fact *circular*, meaning you end up back where you started. What, then, could possibly be the point of hiking?

The answer, of course, is that hiking is not a means to an end, but an end in itself. Hiking forces you to be present, to be aware of your surroundings, lest you trip and fall flat on your face. This is the first part of what makes the experience so enchanting—for the briefest of moments, your mind forces you to forget everything else about your life, and one feels as though one is separate from the fabric of society itself; separate, in a state of nature, away from the hustle and bustle of city life.

Are there any sports that make you feel as I do about hiking? Feel free to let me know at jakes-blog.selection732@silomails.com.
